title: AMS: AEIOUTIPS Pair Match
subtitle: Match each clinical finding to its primary AEIOUTIPS cause.

round_nudges.prompt: Based on those matches — what do you treat first if you're unsure of the cause?
round_nudges.text: Treat the most immediately reversible cause first. Glucose check and dextrose for insulin, high-flow O₂ for hypoxia, naloxone + airway for overdose. When the cause is unclear, these three interventions are fast, low-risk, and diagnostically useful.

round_nudges.prompt: What changes your field approach most dramatically for these causes?
round_nudges.text: Scene clues change everything: empty medication bottles (psychiatric, overdose), farm or chemical odors (toxins), mechanism of injury (trauma), skin color and temperature (temperature, shock). Build the differential from what you see, smell, and measure — not just what dispatch tells you.

pairs.finding: Alcohol odor, ataxia, horizontal nystagmus — found down call
pairs.explanation: Alcohol odor, ataxia, and nystagmus indicate acute intoxication. In chronic alcoholics, thiamine deficiency is a concurrent risk — but do not delay dextrose for confirmed hypoglycemia to give thiamine first. Treat hypoglycemia immediately; thiamine is a secondary consideration if Wernicke's is suspected.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Known epileptic on anticonvulsants, reports recurrent blackout episodes since teens
pairs.explanation: Breakthrough seizures in a known epileptic usually indicate subtherapeutic levels, sleep deprivation, or fever. Field treatment is supportive — protect airway, position laterally, transport. Benzodiazepines if active seizing.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: AMS with muscle cramps after 3 days of vomiting; patient takes high-dose diuretics
pairs.explanation: Vomiting and diuretics both cause hypokalemia and hyponatremia. Severe electrolyte derangements impair neuromuscular function and cerebral activity. IV access and early transport are the field priorities.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Sudden diaphoresis, tremor, confusion — finger-stick glucose 38 mg/dL
pairs.explanation: Glucose 38 mg/dL confirms hypoglycemia. Administer dextrose IV or glucagon IM if no IV access. Expect rapid improvement. Failure to improve after glucose should prompt consideration of concurrent causes — especially stroke or overdose.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Fever 40°C, nuchal rigidity, photophobia — 22-year-old college student
pairs.explanation: Fever + neck stiffness + photophobia is bacterial meningitis until proven otherwise. Time-critical: IV access, rapid transport. Definitive treatment is in-hospital antibiotics. Do not delay transport for extensive field assessment.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Pinpoint pupils, RR 6 per minute — responds to 0.4 mg IV naloxone
pairs.explanation: Miosis + respiratory depression + naloxone response confirms the opioid toxidrome. Naloxone duration (30–90 min) may be shorter than the opioid — reassess frequently and always transport. Re-sedation after naloxone is a common field complication.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Central cyanosis, SpO₂ 84% on room air — near-drowning victim
pairs.explanation: Post-submersion hypoxia with SpO₂ below 90% requires immediate high-flow O₂ and airway management. Submersion victims can deteriorate hours later from aspiration injury and post-submersion pulmonary edema — transport all submersion victims even if they appear to recover on scene.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Ammonia-like breath, asterixis — dialysis patient who missed 3 sessions
pairs.explanation: Asterixis (flapping tremor) and uremic fetor in a missed-dialysis patient is uremic encephalopathy. No field intervention reverses uremia. Priorities: airway management, IV access, emergent transport for dialysis.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Found down with scalp laceration, GCS 10 — reported assault
pairs.explanation: Scalp lacerations bleed extensively and can mask the extent of TBI. GCS 10 = moderate brain injury. Assume c-spine injury until imaging excludes it. Airway management and controlled transport are field priorities.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Core temp 30°C, bradycardia, shivering stopped — 3 hours in the cold
pairs.explanation: Loss of shivering at core temp below 30°C signals severe hypothermia. The cold myocardium is prone to VF — handle gently. Active external rewarming and warmed humidified O₂ when available. Cardiac monitoring mandatory.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Miosis, bradycardia, bronchospasm, excessive secretions — farm chemical exposure
pairs.explanation: SLUDGE: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis + bradycardia and bronchoconstriction = organophosphate poisoning. Antidote is atropine — titrate to drying secretions, not heart rate.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Agitated, disorganized speech, hearing voices — glucose normal, SpO₂ 99%, no trauma
pairs.explanation: Psychiatric AMS diagnosis requires excluding organic causes first. Normal glucose, O₂, vitals, and no trauma history support a psychiatric etiology — but always consider stimulant toxicity, which can look identical.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Sudden right facial droop, arm drift, slurred speech — last normal 45 min ago
pairs.explanation: FAST criteria: Face, Arm, Speech, Time. Last known normal 45 minutes ago is the key field data point. IV access, confirm time, transport to the nearest stroke center. Field stroke scales (Cincinnati, LAMS) help identify large-vessel occlusion candidates.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: BP 78/50, HR 132, pale, diaphoretic, confused — no trauma, fever, or ingestion
pairs.explanation: Hemodynamic shock without an obvious cause: consider sepsis, internal hemorrhage (occult GI bleed, ruptured aortic aneurysm), or cardiogenic shock. IV access, position, O₂, and rapid transport. The cause determines definitive treatment, which is hospital-based.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

pairs.finding: Urinary incontinence, tongue bite marks, improving confusion over 5 minutes
pairs.explanation: Urinary incontinence + tongue bite + gradual improvement over minutes is classic postictal state. Field priorities: recovery position, airway protection, O₂, IV access. Transport for first-time seizure, prolonged seizure (>5 min), or slow/incomplete recovery.
pairs.hint: Match the finding to the system it threatens first: oxygenation, glucose, perfusion, toxin exposure, trauma, temperature, seizure, stroke, or renal/metabolic clearance.

action_cases.presentation: Adult found confused, pale, diaphoretic, and combative. Bystander reports insulin use. No trauma is visible.
choices: Check blood glucose immediately
Begin stroke destination decision first
Administer naloxone before assessment
Request psychiatric evaluation only
action_cases.hint: Pick the fastest reversible cause that matches diaphoresis and insulin history.
action_cases.feedback: Hypoglycemia is rapidly reversible and time-sensitive. Confirm BGL early before anchoring on stroke, intoxication, or psychiatric causes.

action_cases.presentation: Patient is obtunded with pinpoint pupils and respirations of 6/min after being found near drug paraphernalia.
choices: Support ventilation and administer naloxone per protocol
Wait for a full SAMPLE history
Give oral glucose first
Treat as psychiatric agitation
action_cases.correct: Support ventilation and administer naloxone per protocol
action_cases.hint: Treat the life threat before debating the exact substance.
action_cases.feedback: Respiratory depression is the immediate killer in opioid overdose. Ventilate first and use naloxone per scope/protocol.

action_cases.presentation: College student has fever, photophobia, neck stiffness, and worsening confusion.
choices: Use PPE, minimize delays, and transport rapidly for sepsis/meningitis care
Wait on scene for fever to decrease
Treat as simple intoxication
Administer naloxone as the first priority
action_cases.correct: Use PPE, minimize delays, and transport rapidly for sepsis/meningitis care
action_cases.hint: Think infection control and time-sensitive definitive care.
action_cases.feedback: Fever plus meningeal signs and AMS is high risk. EMS priorities are protection, supportive care, IV/access as appropriate, and rapid transport.

action_cases.presentation: Near-drowning patient is confused with central cyanosis and SpO2 84% on room air.
choices: Correct hypoxia with high-flow oxygen and airway support
Perform a stroke scale before oxygen
Focus on psychiatric causes
Delay treatment until lung sounds are charted
action_cases.correct: Correct hypoxia with high-flow oxygen and airway support
action_cases.hint: The monitor and color already name the immediate threat.
action_cases.feedback: Hypoxia is a primary reversible AMS cause. Oxygenation and ventilation take priority.

action_cases.presentation: Found-down patient has scalp bleeding, GCS 10, and an unknown mechanism.
choices: Protect C-spine, manage airway risk, control bleeding, and transport
Assume intoxication until proven otherwise
Have the patient ambulate to test balance
Wait for family history before immobilization decisions
action_cases.correct: Protect C-spine, manage airway risk, control bleeding, and transport
action_cases.hint: Mechanism is unknown and GCS is abnormal. What can worsen during transport?
action_cases.feedback: Traumatic AMS requires spinal/airway vigilance, hemorrhage control, and rapid transport while avoiding anchoring on intoxication.

action_cases.presentation: Dialysis patient missed three treatments and is confused with ammonia-like breath and flapping hand tremor.
choices: Support ABCs, monitor for instability, and transport for dialysis-capable care
Attempt oral hydration on scene
Give naloxone first
Treat as anxiety if oxygen is normal
action_cases.correct: Support ABCs, monitor for instability, and transport for dialysis-capable care
action_cases.hint: Ask whether EMS can definitively reverse the cause in the field.
action_cases.feedback: Uremic encephalopathy is not field-reversible. Support ABCs, monitor, and move toward definitive dialysis-capable care.

action_cases.presentation: Cold-exposure patient is confused, bradycardic, and no longer shivering after hours outside.
choices: Handle gently, prevent further heat loss, monitor rhythm, and begin approved rewarming
Ambulate the patient to generate heat
Give ice water to assess swallowing
Treat bradycardia with aggressive movement first
action_cases.correct: Handle gently, prevent further heat loss, monitor rhythm, and begin approved rewarming
action_cases.hint: Think about the cold myocardium and what rough handling can trigger.
action_cases.feedback: Severe hypothermia creates dysrhythmia risk. Gentle handling, insulation/rewarming, oxygenation, monitoring, and transport are key.

action_cases.presentation: Patient has sudden facial droop, arm drift, slurred speech, and last-known-well 45 minutes ago.
choices: Confirm last-known-well, perform stroke screen, notify/transport to stroke-capable destination
Give oral glucose without checking BGL
Wait for symptoms to resolve
Treat as psychiatric because speech is abnormal
action_cases.correct: Confirm last-known-well, perform stroke screen, notify/transport to stroke-capable destination
action_cases.hint: The time window is the operational clue.
action_cases.feedback: Stroke care depends on time and destination. Confirm last-known-well, screen, notify, and transport appropriately while still checking reversible mimics.
